topics.title: Trie & Advanced Data Structures
topics.story: Google processes 8.5 billion searches per day. For autocomplete to work in milliseconds, Google doesn't search through a dictionary — it uses a Trie (prefix tree). Every letter you type narrows down the branch. 'goo' → instantly arrives at all words starting with 'goo'. A Trie trades space for O(L) time (L = word length), making prefix queries lightning fast regardless of dictionary size.
topics.analogy: A Trie is like a physical filing cabinet organized by letter: drawer A → folders Ab, Ac, Ad... folder Ab → files Aba, Abb, Abc. Finding any word means opening drawers and folders corresponding to its letters — never looking at irrelevant branches.
topics.memoryAnchor: TRIE = reTrieval. Each node has up to 26 children (one per letter). isEnd flag marks complete words. Insert/Search/StartsWith all run in O(L) where L = word length.
topics.firstPrinciples: A trie is an n-ary tree where each path from root to a marked node represents a word. Space: O(alphabet_size * average_key_length * number_of_keys) — can be huge, often compressed. Time: O(L) for all operations. Each node stores children[26] + isEnd flag. Used for autocomplete, spell-check, IP routing (bit-wise trie).
topics.bruteForce: For prefix search without trie: scan all words, check if they start with the prefix — O(total_chars * query_count). For word search in 2D grid without trie: BFS/DFS from each cell for each word — O(words * cells * 4^L).
topics.optimizedApproach: Trie for O(L) prefix search. Word Search II: insert all words into a trie, then single DFS traversal marks all found words simultaneously (vs separate DFS for each word). Segment Tree for range queries with point updates in O(log n). Fenwick Tree for prefix sums with updates in O(log n).
topics.whenToUse: Prefix matching (autocomplete, word suggestion). Count words with given prefix. Search for multiple words simultaneously (Word Search II). IP routing tables (binary trie). Segment Tree: range min/max/sum with point updates. Fenwick Tree: prefix sums with updates (simpler than segment tree for this use case).

examples.title: Trie — Insert, Search, and StartsWith
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":9,"lineCode":"insert('apple')","variables":{"trie":"root","word":"apple"},"phase":"INSERT","annotation":"Insert 'apple': traverse/create nodes for a→p→p→l→e. Mark e.isEnd=true."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"insert('app')","variables":{"trie":"root→a→p→p(isEnd=true)","word":"app"},"phase":"INSERT","annotation":"Insert 'app': nodes a,p,p already exist. Mark second p.isEnd=true. Trie shared prefix structure."},{"line":20,"lineCode":"search('apple')","variables":{"word":"apple","path":"root→a→p→p→l→e"},"phase":"SEARCH","annotation":"Search 'apple': traverse a→p→p→l→e. e.isEnd=true → found!"},{"line":20,"lineCode":"search('app')","variables":{"word":"app","path":"root→a→p→p","isEnd":true},"phase":"SEARCH","annotation":"Search 'app': traverse a→p→p. p.isEnd=true → found! Both 'app' and 'apple' coexist sharing the same a→p→p nodes."},{"line":29,"lineCode":"startsWith('ap')","variables":{"prefix":"ap","path":"root→a→p","result":true},"phase":"PREFIX","annotation":"startsWith 'ap': traverse a→p. Node exists → true. No isEnd check needed for prefix search."},{"line":20,"lineCode":"search('apt')","variables":{"word":"apt","result":false},"phase":"SEARCH","annotation":"Search 'apt': root→a→p→t=null. Return false immediately. O(L) where L=word length."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["root"] --> B["a"]
    B --> C["p"]
    C --> D["p (isEnd=true: 'app')"] 
    D --> E["l"]
    E --> F["e (isEnd=true: 'apple')"]
    A --> G["b"]
    G --> H["a"]
    H --> I["t (isEnd=true: 'bat')"]
    style D fill:#fff3cd
    style F fill:#d4edda
    style I fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
CLASS Trie:
  root = TrieNode()  // each node has children[26] + isEnd flag

  insert(word):
    node = root
    FOR char c in word:
      IF node.children[c-'a'] == null:
        node.children[c-'a'] = new TrieNode()
      node = node.children[c-'a']
    node.isEnd = true

  search(word):
    node = traversePrefix(word)
    RETURN node != null AND node.isEnd

  startsWith(prefix):
    RETURN traversePrefix(prefix) != null
examples.explanation: Trie (prefix tree): each path from root to a node spells out a string. Shared prefixes → shared paths → O(L) insert/search where L=word length (vs O(L×n) for linear scan over n words). The 26-child array gives O(1) child lookup by character. isEnd distinguishes complete words from prefixes. Space: O(total characters in all inserted words).
examples.realWorldUse: Autocomplete (Google search suggestions: all words sharing typed prefix). Spell checker. IP routing (longest prefix match). Dictionary word games. T9 predictive text. Contacts search by prefix. Word Square generation. The Trie structure powers: autocomplete, spell-check, phone book, DNS lookup, router tables.

examples.title: Count Words With Prefix — Trie with Count Field
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":7,"lineCode":"insert('apple')","variables":{"root.count":1,"path":"a(1)→p(1)→p(1)→l(1)→e(1)"},"phase":"INSERT","annotation":"Insert 'apple': increment count at every node along the path. Each count = number of words passing through."},{"line":7,"lineCode":"insert('app')","variables":{"root.count":2,"path":"a(2)→p(2)→p(2)→l(1)→e(1)"},"phase":"INSERT","annotation":"Insert 'app': root.count=2, a.count=2, p.count=2, second_p.count=2. l and e stay at 1."},{"line":7,"lineCode":"insert('april')","variables":{"root.count":3,"a.count":3,"p.count":3},"phase":"INSERT","annotation":"Insert 'april': a.count=3, p.count=3. But then r,i,l are new nodes with count=1."},{"line":19,"lineCode":"countPrefix('ap')","variables":{"prefix":"ap","node.count":3},"phase":"QUERY","annotation":"Traverse to 'p' node. p.count=3 → 3 words start with 'ap': apple, app, april."},{"line":19,"lineCode":"countPrefix('app')","variables":{"prefix":"app","node.count":2},"phase":"QUERY","annotation":"Traverse to second 'p'. count=2 → 2 words start with 'app': apple, app. O(L) per query!"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["root count=3"] --> B["'a' count=3"]
    B --> C["'p' count=3"]
    C --> D["'p' count=2 (app, apple)"]
    C --> E["'r' count=1 (april)"]
    D --> F["'l' count=1"]
    D --> G["isEnd (app)"] 
    F --> H["'e' count=1 isEnd (apple)"]
    E --> I["'i'→'l' isEnd (april)"]
    style D fill:#fff3cd
examples.pseudocode:
MODIFICATION: add 'count' field to each TrieNode

insert(word):
  node = root
  node.count++       // root counts all words
  FOR char c in word:
    create child if missing
    node = node.ch[c]
    node.count++     // increment at each character

countPrefix(prefix):
  node = root
  FOR char c in prefix:
    IF no child for c: RETURN 0
    node = node.ch[c]
  RETURN node.count  // all words passing through = all with this prefix
examples.explanation: Augment each trie node with a `count` field incremented during every insert. After inserting all words, node.count = number of words whose prefix passes through that node = number of words starting with that prefix. countPrefix traverses to the prefix endpoint and reads count directly — O(L) per query regardless of dictionary size. Alternative: DFS from prefix node to count isEnd nodes (O(L + results)).
examples.realWorldUse: Autocomplete with suggestion counts (show 'apple — 1,234 searches'). Logging prefix statistics (how many log lines start with ERROR, WARN, INFO). DNS analytics (how many requests for *.google.com). Word frequency prefix queries. Type-ahead search hit counting. Any system that needs to answer 'how many X start with Y?' in O(L).

examples.title: Segment Tree — Range Sum with Point Update
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":5,"lineCode":"build(nums, 0, 0, n-1);","variables":{"nums":"[1,3,5,7,9,11]","n":6},"phase":"BUILD","annotation":"Build segment tree for [1,3,5,7,9,11]. Internal nodes = sum of children. Leaves = original values."},{"line":17,"lineCode":"tree[node] = tree[2*node+1] + tree[2*node+2];","variables":{"tree":"[36,9,27,4,5,16,11,...]","root":36},"phase":"BUILD","annotation":"Root=36 (sum of all). Left child=9 (sum [1,3,5]), right=27 (sum [7,9,11])."},{"line":22,"lineCode":"update(idx=3, val=10, ...)","variables":{"idx":3,"oldVal":7,"newVal":10},"phase":"UPDATE","annotation":"Update index 3 (value 7→10). Walk down to leaf, update. Recompute sums up the path: O(log n)."},{"line":29,"lineCode":"query(l=1, r=3, ...)","variables":{"l":1,"r":3,"result":18},"phase":"QUERY","annotation":"Sum of indices 1-3 (values 3,5,10 after update): result=18. Recursively split and combine: O(log n)."},{"line":30,"lineCode":"if (r < start || end < l) return 0;","variables":{"pruned":"out-of-range nodes skipped"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Query visits O(log n) nodes. Complete overlap → return immediately. Partial overlap → recurse both sides."}]
examples.pseudocode:
// Segment Tree: range queries + point updates in O(log n)

build(nums, node, start, end):
  IF start == end: tree[node] = nums[start]; RETURN
  mid = (start+end)/2
  build(nums, left_child, start, mid)
  build(nums, right_child, mid+1, end)
  tree[node] = tree[left] + tree[right]  // merge

update(idx, val, node, start, end):
  IF start == end: tree[node] = val; RETURN
  IF idx in left half: update left child
  ELSE: update right child
  tree[node] = tree[left] + tree[right]  // recompute

query(l, r, node, start, end):
  IF no overlap: RETURN 0
  IF full overlap: RETURN tree[node]
  RETURN query(left) + query(right)
examples.explanation: Segment tree: binary tree where each node stores the aggregate (sum/min/max) of a contiguous array range. Build: O(n). Query and update: O(log n) — at each level, a range query touches at most 2 nodes per level. Compare to prefix sum: O(1) query but O(n) update. Segment tree: O(log n) for both. Use when you need BOTH efficient range queries AND point updates.
examples.realWorldUse: Range sum queries with updates (bank transaction totals). Range minimum/maximum (sliding window extremes). Interval tree queries (overlapping intervals). Game leaderboard: range rank queries + score updates. Competitive programming (most tree problems). Database columnar aggregations. Stock price range max/min with live updates.

examples.title: Fenwick Tree (BIT) — Prefix Sum with O(log n) Update
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":11,"lineCode":"update(3, 5);","variables":{"i":3,"delta":5,"binary_3":"011"},"phase":"UPDATE","annotation":"update(3, 5): i=3 (011). LSB=1. tree[3]+=5. i+=1 → i=4 (100). tree[4]+=5. i+=4 → i=8. tree[8]+=5. Propagates up."},{"line":11,"lineCode":"update(2, 3);","variables":{"i":2,"delta":3,"tree":"[0,0,3,5,8,0,0,0,8]"},"phase":"UPDATE","annotation":"update(2, 3): i=2 (010). LSB=2. tree[2]+=3. i+=2 → i=4. tree[4]+=3. i+=4 → i=8. tree[8]+=3."},{"line":17,"lineCode":"query(4);","variables":{"i":4,"sum":0,"binary_4":"100"},"phase":"QUERY","annotation":"query(4): prefix sum [1..4]. i=4 (100). sum+=tree[4]=11. i-=4 → i=0. Return 11."},{"line":17,"lineCode":"query(3);","variables":{"i":3,"path":"3→2→0","sum":8},"phase":"QUERY","annotation":"query(3): i=3(011). sum+=tree[3]=5. i-=1 → i=2(010). sum+=tree[2]=3. i-=2 → i=0. Return 8 = sum[1..3]."},{"line":24,"lineCode":"rangeQuery(2, 4);","variables":{"result":"query(4)-query(1)=11-0=11"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"rangeQuery(2,4)=query(4)-query(1). O(log n) per operation. Simpler than segment tree for prefix sums."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["tree[1..8] — stores partial sums"] --> B["update(i, delta)"]
    B --> C["i += LSB(i)\npropagate up"]
    C --> D["O(log n) nodes updated"]
    A --> E["query(i)"]
    E --> F["i -= LSB(i)\naccumulate down"]
    F --> G["O(log n) reads"]
    style D fill:#d4edda
    style G fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
// Fenwick Tree: prefix sums + updates in O(log n)
// Key operation: i & (-i) = lowest set bit of i

update(i, delta):        // 1-indexed
  WHILE i <= n:
    tree[i] += delta
    i += i & (-i)         // jump to next responsible index

query(i):                // prefix sum [1..i]
  sum = 0
  WHILE i > 0:
    sum += tree[i]
    i -= i & (-i)         // jump to parent coverage
  RETURN sum

rangeQuery(l, r):
  RETURN query(r) - query(l-1)
examples.explanation: Fenwick Tree (Binary Indexed Tree) exploits binary representation: each index i is responsible for a range of length equal to its lowest set bit (LSB). update propagates by adding LSB (moves to next responsible ancestor). query accumulates by subtracting LSB (moves to next coverage block). O(log n) updates and queries, O(n) space. Simpler to implement than segment tree for prefix sums — but can only support operations that have inverses (sum but not min/max).
examples.realWorldUse: Competitive programming: most 'prefix sum with updates' problems. Order statistics (count elements ≤ x in a dynamic array). Inversion count. Frequency counting with prefix queries. Rank-based operations in databases. The Fenwick tree is the space-efficient, simpler alternative to segment tree when you only need prefix aggregates.

examples.title: Word Search II — Trie + Backtracking (DFS on Grid)
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":7,"lineCode":"for (String w : words) insert(root, w);","variables":{"words":"['eat','tea','tan','ate','nat','bat']","trie":"built from all 6 words"},"phase":"BUILD","annotation":"Build trie from all words. Trie prefix-prunes the DFS: if current path isn't a trie prefix, stop immediately."},{"line":12,"lineCode":"dfs(board, r, c, root, ...)","variables":{"board":"[['e','a','t'],['a','n','a'],['t','e','t']]","start":"(0,0)='e'"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Start DFS from every cell. At (0,0)='e': trie has 'e' → continue."},{"line":22,"lineCode":"if (next == null) return; // PRUNE","variables":{"path":"'e'","next":"trie node for 'e'"},"phase":"DFS","annotation":"'e' is a valid prefix. Continue to neighbors. 'ea'→'eat' is in trie."},{"line":27,"lineCode":"if (next.isEnd) found.add(path.toString());","variables":{"path":"'eat'","found":"{'eat'}"},"phase":"FOUND","annotation":"'eat' path followed: e(0,0)→a(0,1)→t(0,2). next.isEnd=true → add 'eat'."},{"line":22,"lineCode":"pruning: 'b' not in trie branch under current path","variables":{"pruning":"millions of DFS paths cut"},"phase":"PRUNE","annotation":"Key win over naive: single DFS from a cell checks ALL words simultaneously. If path 'xyz' isn't any word prefix, the entire subtree is pruned."},{"line":1,"lineCode":"done","variables":{"found":"{'eat','tea','tan','ate','nat','bat'}"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"All 6 words found. O(m×n × 4^L + total_chars) vs O(m×n × 4^L × words) for naive approach."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["Build Trie from all words"] --> B["DFS from every cell"]
    B --> C{"board[r][c] in trie.children?"}
    C -- No --> D["PRUNE: skip entire subtree"]
    C -- Yes --> E["Advance trie node"]
    E --> F{"node.isEnd?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Add word to results"]
    F -- No --> H["Continue DFS in 4 dirs"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Backtrack: restore board + path"]
    style D fill:#f8d7da
    style G fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION findWords(board, words):
  trie = buildTrie(words)

  FOR each cell (r, c):
    dfs(board, r, c, trie.root, path='')

  RETURN found

FUNCTION dfs(board, r, c, trieNode, path):
  IF out of bounds OR visited: RETURN
  ch = board[r][c]
  next = trieNode.children[ch]
  IF next == null: RETURN  // PRUNE!

  path += ch; board[r][c] = '#'  // mark
  IF next.isEnd: found.add(path)
  FOR each direction: dfs(next, ...)
  path.removeLast(); board[r][c] = ch  // restore
examples.explanation: Combines two patterns: Trie for simultaneous prefix matching + DFS backtracking for path exploration. Key optimization: a single DFS from cell (r,c) checks ALL words at once by following the trie. If no word starts with the current path, the ENTIRE subtree is pruned — far more efficient than running separate DFS for each word. The trie node follows the board exploration, acting as a constraint that immediately prunes dead-end paths.
examples.realWorldUse: Boggle AI solver (finds all valid words simultaneously). Crossword generation. Multi-pattern string matching on 2D grids. Genomics: finding multiple DNA motifs in a 2D alignment matrix. The pattern of 'build trie first, then use trie as DFS constraint' is a general technique for any multi-pattern search problem.

problems.title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
problems.description:
Implement a Trie with `insert(word)`, `search(word)`, and `startsWith(prefix)`.

**Example:**
trie.insert("apple"); trie.search("apple") => true; trie.search("app") => false; trie.startsWith("app") => true

**Key insight:** Each node has a char map to children and an `isEnd` flag. Search and prefix differ only in whether isEnd matters.
problems.hint1: Each node in the trie has 26 children (one per letter) and a flag marking if it's the end of a word.
problems.hint2: insert: walk the trie, create nodes for missing letters, set isEnd=true at the last character. search: walk the trie, return node.isEnd at the last character. startsWith: walk and return true if all chars are found.
problems.hint3: The difference between search and startsWith: search requires isEnd=true at the final node. startsWith just needs all prefix nodes to exist.
problems.testCases: [{"input":"insert('apple'), search('apple'), search('app'), startsWith('app'), insert('app'), search('app')","output":"true, false, true, true"}]
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ word.length, prefix.length ≤ 2000 | Lowercase English letters | At most 3*10^4 calls total
problems.editorial: All operations O(L) where L = word length. TrieNode stores children[26] (sparse — can use HashMap for larger alphabets). search vs startsWith: both traverse the trie, but search additionally requires isEnd=true at the final node.

problems.title: Word Search II
problems.description:
Given a 2D board and a list of words, find all words that exist in the board (adjacent cells, no reuse).

**Example:**
board=[[o,a,a,n],[e,t,a,e],[i,h,k,r],[i,f,l,v]], words=["oath","pea","eat","rain"]
=> ["eat","oath"]

**Key insight:** Build Trie from words, then DFS from each cell. Prune branches not in Trie.
problems.hint1: Running separate DFS for each word is O(words * cells * 4^L) — too slow. Can one traversal find all words?
problems.hint2: Insert all words into a trie. DFS from each cell — follow the trie simultaneously. If the current cell's letter doesn't have a trie child, prune immediately.
problems.hint3: DFS: board[r][c] → follow trie node's child for that letter. If node.isEnd, found a word. Mark cell as visited by temporarily setting to '#'. Undo after recursion.
problems.constraints: m == board.length | n == board[i].length | 1 ≤ m, n ≤ 12 | 1 ≤ words.length ≤ 3*10^4 | 1 ≤ words[i].length ≤ 10
problems.editorial: Trie-guided DFS: O(m * n * 4^maxWordLen) but heavily pruned. Storing the word string at the trie leaf avoids re-traversal for reporting. Setting node.word=null after finding prevents duplicates. Mark board cell '#' for visited, restore after backtrack.

problems.title: Range Sum Query - Mutable (Fenwick Tree)
problems.description:
Support point update and range sum query on an array using a Fenwick Tree (BIT).

**Example:**
nums=[1,3,5,7,9,11]
sumRange(0,2) => 9 (1+3+5)
update(1, 2) // nums[1] += 2 => nums=[1,5,5,7,9,11]
sumRange(0,2) => 11

**Key insight:** BIT stores partial sums. Update in O(log n) via index += index & (-index). Query via prefix sum.
problems.hint1: Brute force: update O(1), query O(n). Prefix sum: query O(1), update O(n). Can you get O(log n) for both?
problems.hint2: Fenwick Tree (Binary Indexed Tree): stores partial sums in a clever array. Each index i is responsible for a range of length i & (-i) (lowest set bit of i).
problems.hint3: update(i, delta): add delta to tree[i], tree[i + (i&-i)], ... (move up by clearing lowest bit). query(i): sum tree[i] + tree[i - (i&-i)] + ... (move down by clearing lowest bit).
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ nums.length ≤ 3*10^4 | -100 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 100 | 0 ≤ i ≤ j < nums.length | At most 3*10^4 calls total
problems.editorial: Fenwick Tree (BIT): O(log n) both update and query. Key operation: i & (-i) = lowest set bit of i. Update propagates up; query accumulates going down. 1-indexed internally. More space-efficient than segment tree for this specific use case (prefix sum + point update).

problems.title: Design Add and Search Words (Wildcard Trie)
problems.description:
Implement a trie supporting `addWord(word)` and `search(word)` where '.' matches any letter.

**Example:**
wd.addWord("bad"); wd.addWord("dad"); wd.addWord("mad");
wd.search("pad") => false
wd.search(".ad") => true
wd.search("b..") => true

**Key insight:** On '.', recurse into all children. Regular characters follow normal Trie traversal.
problems.hint1: Regular trie handles normal search. How does '.' complicate things?
problems.hint2: When you hit a '.', you can't follow just one child — you must try ALL 26 children. This makes '.' search non-deterministic.
problems.hint3: Recursively search: for regular char, follow the single child (or return false). For '.', try all non-null children recursively — return true if any succeeds.
problems.testCases: [{"input":"addWord('bad'), addWord('dad'), addWord('mad'), search('pad'), search('.ad'), search('b..')","output":"false, true, true"}]
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ word.length ≤ 25 | Lowercase letters and '.'. At most 3 dots in search queries | At most 10^4 calls total
problems.editorial: Extend trie with recursive search handling '.' by branching into all 26 children. Worst case: all '.' query scans entire trie — O(26^n) but bounded by actual trie size. Normal char queries: O(L). Classic extension of basic trie to handle wildcards.

problems.title: Replace Words
problems.description:
Replace each word in a sentence with the shortest root from a dictionary that is a prefix of that word.

**Example:**
replaceWords(["cat","bat","rat"], "the cattle was rattled by the battery")
=> "the cat was rat by the bat"

**Key insight:** Build Trie from roots. For each word, search Trie for the shortest matching prefix.
problems.hint1: For each word in the sentence, you need to find the shortest dictionary root that is a prefix of that word.
problems.hint2: Build a trie from all dictionary roots. For each sentence word, traverse the trie character by character — the first node marked as a root end is the shortest root.
problems.hint3: Insert all roots into the trie, mark isEnd. For each sentence word, walk the trie until you hit isEnd (return the root so far) or exhaust the trie (return the original word).
problems.testCases: [{"input":"dictionary=['cat','bat','rat'], sentence='the cattle was rattled by the battery'","output":"'the cat was rat by the bat'"}]
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ dictionary.length ≤ 1000 | 1 ≤ dictionary[i].length ≤ 100 | 1 ≤ sentence.length ≤ 10^6 | Lowercase English letters and spaces
problems.editorial: Build trie from all roots. For each sentence word, walk the trie — the first isRoot=true node gives the shortest root (stop early). If the trie branch ends before finding a root, use the original word. O(total dictionary chars + total sentence chars).
problems.sampleInput: roots=[cat,bat,rat], sentence="the cattle was rattled by the battery"
problems.sampleOutput: "the cat was rat by the bat"

problems.title: Longest Word in Dictionary
problems.description:
Return the longest word in a dictionary that can be built character by character (each prefix must also exist in the dictionary).

**Example:**
longestWord(["w","wo","wor","worl","world"]) => "world"
longestWord(["a","banana","app","appl","ap","apply","apple"]) => "apple"

**Key insight:** Build Trie, BFS/DFS checking that each node's word was built step by step.
problems.hint1: A word can be 'built' if every prefix of it (of length 1, 2, ..., len-1) also exists in the array.
problems.hint2: Insert all words into a trie. A word is valid if every node along its path has isEnd=true (meaning that prefix also exists as a word in the array).
problems.hint3: BFS/DFS from the root: only explore children where the child's node has isEnd=true. Track the longest (then lexicographically smallest) word found.
problems.editorial: Insert all words into trie, storing the word string at leaf. BFS only through nodes that have isEnd=true — this guarantees every prefix is a valid word. Update result when we find a longer (or lex-smaller same-length) word. O(total chars) time and space.

problems.title: Map Sum Pairs
problems.description:
Insert `(key, val)` pairs into a Trie-based map and sum all values whose keys have a given prefix.

**Example:**
mapSum.insert("apple", 3); mapSum.insert("app", 2);
mapSum.sum("ap") => 5 (apple:3 + app:2)
mapSum.sum("b") => 0

**Key insight:** Each Trie node stores a value. `sum(prefix)` = DFS summing all values below the prefix node.
problems.hint1: You need O(L) insert and O(L + result) sum query. A hash map can handle insert in O(L) but sum requires scanning all keys — O(n*L).
problems.hint2: Use a trie where each node stores the sum of all values of words passing through it. On insert, update every node along the key's path.
problems.hint3: If re-inserting an existing key with a new value, compute the delta (newVal - oldVal) and add delta to all nodes along the path (not newVal). Store original key→val in a map to compute the delta.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ key.length, prefix.length ≤ 50 | 0 ≤ val ≤ 1000 | At most 50 calls total
problems.editorial: Store prefix sums directly in each trie node. On insert, compute delta = newVal - previousVal for that key (prevents double-counting on re-insert). Add delta to every node on the key's path. sum(prefix): traverse to the prefix endpoint and return its stored sum. O(L) for both operations.

problems.title: Autocomplete System
problems.description:
Design an autocomplete system: given historical sentences with frequencies, return top 3 matching sentences as user types.

**Example:**
system = AutocompleteSystem(["i love you","island","iroman"],[5,3,2])
system.input('i') => ["i love you","island","iroman"]
system.input(' ') => ["i love you"]
system.input('#') => [] (saves new sentence)

**Key insight:** Trie with frequency map at each node. On '#', save the typed sentence.
problems.hint1: You need prefix lookup + ranking by (frequency desc, lex asc). A trie gives fast prefix lookup; how to rank efficiently?
problems.hint2: At each trie node, store a map of sentence → frequency for all sentences passing through. On input(c), traverse to the current prefix node and return the top 3 from its map.
problems.hint3: Use a HashMap<String, Integer> at each node (or globally). For '#', add the typed sentence to the global freq map and trie. Use a min-heap of size 3 or sort the candidates.
problems.testCases: [{"input":"AutocompleteSystem(['i love you','island','iroman','i love leetcode'],[5,3,2,2]), input('i'), input(' '), input('a'), input('#')","output":"['i love you','island','i love leetcode'], ['i love you','i love leetcode'], [], []"}]
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ sentences.length ≤ 100 | 1 ≤ sentences[i].length ≤ 100 | 1 ≤ times[i] ≤ 50 | input called at most 5000 times | Only ASCII printable characters + space
problems.editorial: Each trie node stores a frequency map of all sentences passing through it. insert propagates updates through all nodes on the path. On input(c), traverse to the new prefix node and sort its freq map for top 3. Use ASCII-indexed children (size 128) to handle spaces. '#' finalizes the sentence and inserts it with frequency 1.

problems.title: Range Min Query (Segment Tree)
problems.description:
Answer range minimum queries on a static array using a Segment Tree.

**Example:**
nums=[1,3,5,7,9,11]
queryMin(0,5) => 1
queryMin(1,3) => 3
update(2, 0) // nums[2]=0
queryMin(0,5) => 0

**Key insight:** Segment tree stores min for each range. Build O(n), query and update O(log n).
problems.hint1: Unlike Fenwick Tree (best for prefix sums), Segment Tree handles arbitrary range queries (min, max, sum, GCD) with O(log n) update and query.
problems.hint2: Segment tree: array of size 4*n. tree[1] = root. For node i, left child = 2*i, right child = 2*i+1. Each node stores the min of its range.
problems.hint3: Build: recursively split [l, r] into halves, store min at each node. Query[l,r]: if node's range fully inside [l,r] return its value; if fully outside return INF; else query both children. Update: walk down to the leaf, update, propagate min up.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ nums.length ≤ 10^5 | -10^4 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10^4 | 0 ≤ i < nums.length | 0 ≤ l ≤ r < nums.length
problems.editorial: Segment tree with 4*n nodes. Build O(n). Query and update both O(log n). Key: node i covers range [nl, nr], left child covers [nl, mid], right child [mid+1, nr]. For query: return INF if range fully outside, return tree[node] if fully inside, else recurse both and take min. Generalizes to sum, max, GCD by changing the merge operation.

problems.title: Index Pairs of a String
problems.description:
Find all index pairs [i,j] in a text string that match any string from a list of words.

**Example:**
indexPairs("thestoryofleetcodeandme", ["story","fleet","leetcode"])
=> [[3,7],[10,17],[10,14]]

**Key insight:** Build Trie from words. Scan text starting at each index, following Trie until no match or word end.
problems.hint1: Brute force: for each starting index i, check all substrings starting at i against the word set — O(n^2) substrings, O(L) each = O(n^2 * L).
problems.hint2: Build a trie from the words. For each starting index i in text, walk the trie following text[i], text[i+1], ... — each node with isEnd=true gives an index pair.
problems.hint3: Outer loop: each starting index i (0 to n-1). Inner: traverse trie while characters match, collect [i, j] whenever isEnd=true. Stop when trie has no matching child.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ text.length ≤ 100 | 1 ≤ words.length ≤ 20 | 1 ≤ words[i].length ≤ 50 | Lowercase English letters
problems.editorial: Build trie from all words. For each start index i, walk the trie following text characters — every isEnd=true node yields a valid pair [i, j]. Breaking when a trie child doesn't exist prunes the inner loop early. Results naturally come out sorted. O(n^2) worst case but heavily pruned in practice.

problems.title: Maximum XOR of Two Numbers in an Array (Binary Trie)
problems.description:
Find the maximum XOR of any two numbers in an array using a Binary Trie.

**Example:**
findMaximumXOR([3,10,5,25,2,8]) => 28 (5 XOR 25 = 28)

**Key insight:** Build a Trie of binary representations. For each number, greedily choose the opposite bit at each level to maximize XOR.
problems.hint1: XOR is maximized by choosing bits that differ. For each number, you want to find the number in the array that differs in as many high-order bits as possible.
problems.hint2: Build a binary trie of all numbers (bit by bit from most significant). For each number, greedily traverse the trie choosing the opposite bit at each level.
problems.hint3: Insert all numbers (32-bit, MSB first). For each number, traverse: prefer the branch whose bit is opposite to current number's bit (maximizes XOR at that position).
problems.editorial: Binary trie stores numbers bit by bit (MSB to LSB). For each query number, greedily pick the opposite bit at each level — if the opposite branch exists, we can set that XOR bit to 1. O(n * 32) = O(n) time and space. Array-based trie is faster than pointer-based for this size.

problems.title: Count of Range Sum
problems.description:
Count the number of range sums S(i,j) = nums[i]+...+nums[j] that lie in [lower, upper].

**Example:**
countRangeSum([-2,5,-1], lower=-2, upper=2) => 3
(subarrays: [-2], [-1], [-2,5,-1]=2)

**Key insight:** Prefix sums + merge sort. Count pairs (i,j) where prefix[j] - prefix[i] is in range.
problems.hint1: Brute force O(n^2): compute all prefix sums, check all pairs. Can you count valid pairs faster using prefix sums?
problems.hint2: Let prefix[i] = sum(nums[0..i-1]). Range sum S(i,j) = prefix[j+1] - prefix[i]. We need count of pairs where lower ≤ prefix[j] - prefix[i] ≤ upper, i.e., prefix[j] - upper ≤ prefix[i] ≤ prefix[j] - lower.
problems.hint3: Use merge sort on the prefix sums array. During merge, for each prefix[j] in the right half, use two pointers (lo, hi) on the left half to count valid prefix[i] values in O(n) per level. Total: O(n log n).
problems.editorial: Key insight: convert to prefix sum problem. S(i,j) = prefix[j+1] - prefix[i]. Need pairs where lower ≤ prefix[j] - prefix[i] ≤ upper. Merge sort counts valid pairs across halves in O(n) per merge step using two monotonic pointers (lo, hi) on the left half for each j in right half. Total: O(n log n). Use long[] to avoid integer overflow.
